lesson_title: Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.back: The process of matching function calls to a specific overloaded function
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.front: What are the three possible outcomes when the compiler tries to resolve an overloaded function call?
cards.back:
• A match is found
  • No match is found
  • An ambiguous match is found
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.text: When no match is found for an overloaded function call, the compiler will issue a {{c1::compile error}}.
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.back: When the compiler finds two or more functions that can be matched, but cannot determine which is the best match
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.question: Will this code compile?
cards.answer: No, it will cause an ambiguous match compile error.
cards.explanation: The float argument 5.0f can be converted to either int or double with equal priority, creating an ambiguous match.
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.text: The compiler will prefer an {{c1::exact match}} over a match requiring type conversion.
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.question: Which function is called?
cards.explanation: The argument 5 is an exact match for int, so print(int) is selected over print(double) which would require a conversion.
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.front: What happens when the compiler encounters an ambiguous function call?
cards.back: The compiler will issue a compile error indicating an ambiguous match.
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.question: Will this compile?
cards.answer: No, it will cause an ambiguous match error.
cards.explanation: The literal 0 is of type int, which can be converted to both unsigned int and float with equal priority, creating ambiguity.
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.text: To resolve an ambiguous function call, you can use a {{c1::static_cast}} to explicitly convert the argument to the desired type.
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.concept: How to resolve ambiguous function call errors
cards.explanation:
When you get an ambiguous match error, you can:
  • Use static_cast to explicitly convert the argument to match one overload
  • Use a literal suffix to make the type explicit (e.g., 5.0f for float)
  • Remove or modify one of the ambiguous overloads
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches

cards.front: Why does overload resolution prefer exact matches over matches requiring conversions?
cards.back: To ensure the most appropriate and type-safe function is called, avoiding unnecessary conversions that could lose information or precision.
cards.source: 11.3 - Function overload resolution and ambiguous matches
